result:
# The Weight of Unasked Questions

There's a peculiar kind of silence that settles over a dinner table when everyone decides, collectively and without speaking, to avoid the thing that matters most. You've felt it. The way forks scrape plates a little louder. The way someone comments on the weather with sudden, desperate interest.

I've been thinking about all the conversations we don't have.

My grandmother kept a drawer full of letters she never sent. I found them after she died, rubber-banded together, addressed to people I'd never heard her mention. A sister in another state. A friend from nursing school. A man whose name appeared only once, with no return address written, as if she couldn't decide whether she wanted a response.

I didn't read them. That felt like a line I couldn't uncross. But I've wondered, in the years since, what words she carried alone. What apologies or confessions or simple declarations of love sat folded in that drawer, growing yellow at the edges while she made coffee and watched game shows and lived her quiet life ten feet away from everything she couldn't say.

We tell ourselves there will be time. That's the lie that makes cowardice feel like patience. *Next visit, I'll ask about the war. Next phone call, I'll tell her what she meant to me. Next year, when things are calmer, I'll finally say the true thing.*

The true thing. It sits in your chest like a stone, doesn't it? Sometimes for so long you forget it's there. You build your life around it—your conversations, your careful deflections, your small talk that fills the space where meaning might have lived.

---

I met a man once who'd spent forty years not speaking to his brother. When I asked why, he couldn't quite remember. "Something about our father's business," he said, waving his hand as if swatting a fly. "Money, probably. Or pride. Same thing, really."

His brother had died two months before we spoke. Heart attack, quick and complete. No warning, no window for reconciliation, no final phone call where stubborn old men might have laughed and admitted they'd wasted decades over something neither could now name.

"The thing is," he told me, and here his voice cracked in a way that made me look away, "I can't even remember his voice anymore. Forty years. I can see his face, but when I try to hear him speak, there's nothing there."

Silence has a cost. It compounds, like interest, like grief. Every day you don't say the thing, it becomes harder to say. The not-saying becomes its own truth, its own relationship, its own strange intimacy.

---

But I don't want to write only about regret. That's too easy, and too cruel, and probably not even true. Because sometimes silence is a gift. Sometimes the unasked question is an act of mercy.

My father never asked me about the year I disappeared into myself, the year I stopped calling, stopped showing up, stopped being the person he thought he'd raised. When I finally resurfaced, he simply said, "Good to see you, kid," and handed me a cup of coffee.

Maybe he was afraid of the answer. Maybe he understood that some things need to stay buried for the person who buried them to keep breathing. Or maybe—and this is what I choose to believe—he trusted that if I needed him to know, I would tell him.

That's a different kind of silence. Not absence, but space. Room for someone to become who they need to become without the weight of explanation.

---

I wonder sometimes about the questions we ask too much. The performed curiosity that's really just filling air. "How are you?" we say, and everyone knows the expected answer, and everyone gives it, and we move on having communicated nothing except our mutual agreement to keep things smooth.

What if we asked less, but meant it more?

What if "how are you" became something we only said when we had time to hear the truth, when we were genuinely willing to stand there while someone told us about the test results, the divorce, the 3 AM fears that make sleep impossible?

---

There's a poem I half-remember about two people sitting together in comfortable silence, and how that silence was more intimate than any words could be. I can't find it now—maybe I invented it, or dreamed it, or assembled it from scraps of things I've read over the years.

But I believe in it. The silence that isn't empty, but full. Two people who have said enough to each other, over enough years, that they can simply exist in the same room and feel known.

That's what I want. Not the absence of words, but the freedom from needing them. The understanding that runs beneath language, in the current of shared history, shared meals, shared ordinary days that accumulate into something like a life.

---

My grandmother died on a Tuesday. I wasn't there. I was at work, doing something I can no longer remember, something that surely seemed important at the time.

I still have her letters. I still haven't read them.

Maybe someday I will. Or maybe I'll leave them for someone else to find, to wonder about, to hold as evidence that even the quietest lives contain depths we never see.

We are all, in the end, composed as much of what we don't say as what we do.

The drawer is always there.
